stensola-h: Hanne Stensola was a postdoctoral researcher in the Mainen Lab from 2015 to 2021. Based on the available records, her specific research focus and contributions during her time in the lab are not documented in the current database. Her current position following her departure from the lab is not listed in the available information.

stern-j: Joshua Stern served as a technician in the Mainen Lab from 2022 to 2023, providing technical support for the laboratory's research activities. During his time in the lab, he contributed to the day-to-day operations and experimental work of the group.
stilwell-fonseca-m: Madalena Stilwell Fonseca was a PhD student in the lab from 2013-2020, where she investigated serotonin's role in learning and behavior using electrophysiological and fMRI approaches. Her research focused on how serotonergic stimulation affects learning rates for rewards and waiting behavior, demonstrating that activation of dorsal raphe serotonergic neurons promotes waiting but is not inherently reinforcing. She also contributed to developing functional MRI techniques for studying large-scale brain activity in behaving mice.
sullivan-e: Eric Sullivan was a student in the Mainen Lab from 1999-2000, during the lab's early years at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory. Limited information is available about his specific research contributions or current position.
svoboda-k: Karel Svoboda was a member of the Mainen Lab, where he contributed to research on barrel cortex function, olfactory bulb circuitry, and synaptic plasticity using imaging approaches. His work in the lab focused on understanding neural circuit dynamics and plasticity mechanisms in sensory systems. His current position and affiliation are not specified.
tavanfar-a: Alireza Tavanfar was a postdoc in the Mainen Lab from 2020, focusing on theoretical physics research spanning quantum mechanics, holographic theories, and gravitational physics, with particular expertise in black hole physics and quantum field theory. He is currently a Visiting Scientist in the lab, continuing his research at the intersection of quantum theory and gravity. His work explores fundamental questions about unitarity, quantum information, and the holographic principle in various gravitational contexts.
teixeira-jm: Jose Miguel Teixeira completed his MSc degree as a student in the Mainen Lab from 2019 to 2023. He was awarded his MSc in May 2023. His specific research focus and current position are not detailed in the available records.
tessereau-c: Charline Tessereau is a Visiting Scientist in the Mainen Lab in 2025, working at the intersection of spatial navigation, decision-making, and reinforcement learning. Her research focuses on how the hippocampus represents spatial information during flexible navigation and how prior information influences brain-wide neural activity during decision-making under uncertainty. She has also contributed to understanding team science practices through her involvement with the International Brain Laboratory.
treves-i: Isaac Treves was a student in the Mainen Lab in 2020. Due to the brief duration of his time in the lab, specific details about his research contributions and current position are not available.
turco-k: Katherine Turco was a MSc student in the Mainen Lab from 2012-2013. During her time in the lab, she contributed to the research program investigating neural coding and computation in behavioral paradigms. Her current position and research activities are not available in our records.
uchida-n: Naoshige Uchida was a postdoc in the Mainen Lab from 2000-2006, where he worked on olfactory decision-making. His research focuses on understanding dopamine signaling, reinforcement learning, and decision-making processes, with particular emphasis on how prediction errors and value computations are implemented in neural circuits. He is now a Professor at Harvard University, where he continues to investigate the neural mechanisms underlying learning and behavior.
venturini-r: Rita Venturini was a Visiting Scientist in the Mainen Lab from 2011-2017. Due to limited available information about her specific research contributions and current position, further details about her work and career trajectory are not available at this time.
vertechi-p: Pietro Vertechi completed his PhD in the Mainen Lab from 2013-2020, focusing on computational decision theory and the neural mechanisms underlying foraging behavior. His research examined how serotonin neurons influence decision-making strategies, particularly the balance between exploration and exploitation in probabilistic environments, and investigated the differential contributions of prefrontal cortical areas to inference-based decisions in hidden state tasks. His work combined computational modeling with experimental neuroscience to understand how animals make optimal foraging decisions under uncertainty.
viana-s: Sam Viana was a student in the Mainen Lab in 2011. Due to the brief duration of their time in the lab, limited information is available about their specific research contributions or current position.
vicente-mi: Maria Ines Vicente was a PhD student in the Mainen Lab from 2007-2015, where she worked on projects investigating olfactory cortex and memory as well as voluntary action and free will. Her research contributed to the lab's understanding of neural mechanisms underlying sensory processing and decision-making behaviors.
winter-o: Olivier Winter served as a technician in the Mainen Lab from 2018-2020, where he contributed to the International Brain Laboratory project. His research focuses on developing computational tools and methods for large-scale electrophysiology, including spike sorting algorithms, data architecture systems, and quality metrics for neural recordings across different brain regions and species. He has since departed from the lab and is currently unaffiliated.
xu-n: Ninglong Xu was a postdoc in the Mainen Lab from 2006-2008, during the laboratory's early years at the Champalimaud Centre for the Unknown. His research focuses on neural circuit mechanisms underlying perceptual decision-making, with particular emphasis on cortical and striatal circuits that support sensory categorization and flexible behavior. He is now a Principal Investigator at the Chinese Academy of Sciences, where he continues to investigate how neural ensemble dynamics in motor and sensory systems contribute to adaptive decision-making processes.
zador-a: Based on the limited available data, Anthony Zador is a member of the Mainen Lab working on hippocampal dendritic computation. His research focuses on understanding how dendrites process information within hippocampal circuits. Current details about his specific contributions to the lab and career trajectory are not available in the provided records.
zariwala-h: Hatim Zariwala was a PhD student in the Mainen Lab from 2000-2007, where he worked on olfactory decision-making during the lab's early years at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory. His research focused on understanding how animals process odor information to make behavioral choices. He has since departed from research.
